did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used reinforcement learning (specifically deep Q-learning) and supervised learning models to improve skin cancer diagnosis (melanoma and basal cell carcinoma) and management decisions. The RL model utilized nonuniform rewards based on expert-generated tables to balance benefits and harms of diagnostic errors, resulting in improved sensitivity for melanoma (61.4% to 79.5%) and basal cell carcinoma (79.4% to 87.1%).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The article is a 'Brief Communication' in Nature Medicine that presents original experimental results. It describes the development and validation of a reinforcement learning model for skin cancer diagnosis, including training on a dataset of 10,015 images, testing on an independent set of 1,511 images, and conducting a reader study with 89 dermatologists. The paper reports new quantitative findings, such as improved sensitivity and optimal management rates, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A reinforcement learning model for AI-based decision support in skin cancer
brief_summary: This study investigated whether incorporating human preferences via reinforcement learning could improve AI-based decision support for skin cancer diagnosis. The researchers trained a deep Q-learning model using nonuniform rewards and penalties based on expert-generated tables to balance the benefits and harms of diagnostic errors. The model was trained on a dataset of 10,015 images from the HAM10000 dataset and evaluated on an independent test set of 1,511 images. Compared to a supervised learning baseline, the reinforcement learning model significantly improved sensitivity for melanoma (from 61.4% to 79.5%) and basal cell carcinoma (from 79.4% to 87.1%) while reducing AI overconfidence. A reader study with 89 dermatologists demonstrated that AI support increased correct diagnosis rates by 12.0% and optimal management decisions from 57.4% to 65.3%.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant skin cancer; sensitivity improved from 61.4% to 79.5% with RL model. Included in malignant class for binary scenario.
Malignant skin cancer; sensitivity improved from 79.4% to 87.1% with RL model. Included in malignant class for binary scenario.
Precancerous condition; included in malignant class for binary scenario. Optimal management involves local therapy.
Benign condition; included in benign class for binary scenario. Optimal management involves dismissal.
Benign condition; included in benign class for binary scenario. Optimal management involves dismissal. Most common benign lesion in patient-centered dataset (n=7,213).
Benign condition; included in benign class for binary scenario. Optimal management involves dismissal.
Benign condition; included in benign class for binary scenario. Optimal management involves dismissal.

all_task_types.target: Seven diagnostic categories (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis/intraepidermal carcinoma, benign keratinocytic lesion, melanocytic nevus, dermatofibroma, vascular lesion) and binary malignant vs. benign status.
all_task_types.details: The study trained supervised learning and reinforcement learning models to predict multiclass probabilities for seven skin lesion types using the HAM10000 dataset. The models were evaluated on sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy for diagnosing specific cancer types (melanoma, BCC) and distinguishing malignant from benign lesions. The RL model optimized actions (dismiss, excise, treat locally, monitor) based on predicted class probabilities and expert-defined rewards.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, publicly available via Harvard Dataverse.

use.training: Used to train the supervised learning (SL) model and the reinforcement learning (RL) model. The dataset was split into an 80/20 partition, with the 80% used for training and the 20% used as a validation set.
use.validation: The 20% partition of HAM10000 was used as a validation set to track the evolution of the models.
use.other: Used for external validation of the SL and RL models alongside the ISIC 2018 test set.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018 Challenge Test Set
datasets_used.description: An independent test set of retrospectively collected dermatoscopic images from different sites (Austria, Australia, Turkey, New Zealand, Sweden, Argentina) used for external validation and reader study.

use.testing: Used as an independent test set for the external validation of the SL and RL models and for the reader study involving 89 dermatologists.

datasets_used.name: Patient-Centered Dataset
datasets_used.description: A private dataset of dermatoscopic images collected from the University Department of Dermatology, Medical University of Vienna, and a dermatology practice in Vienna, representing a patient-centered scenario with multiple lesions per patient.
classes_used: benign lesions (nevi, keratinocytic lesions, dermatofibromas, vascular lesions, other benign lesions)

use.training: Used to train and evaluate the RL model for the patient-centered scenario based on a 20-fold cross-validation strategy.
use.other: Used to evaluate management strategies (dismiss, monitor, excise) in a patient-centered context.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To estimate the uncertainty of sensitivity improvements for melanoma and basal cell carcinoma, and the improvement in correct diagnosis rates and optimal management decisions.
To compare the difference in entropy of paired test set predictions between the supervised learning (SL) model and the reinforcement learning (RL) model.
To compare true positive rates (TPRs) for melanomas applying thresholds (SL model) and rewards (RL model) provided by ten experts.
To compare proportions (specifically mentioned in the Statistical Analysis section as general methods for proportions).
For comparisons of continuous data between groups, as appropriate.
To determine statistical significance for reported P values.

hypothesis_testing.test: Wilcoxon signed-rank test, paired t-test, Chi-square tests, McNemar tests
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in the metric being compared between the groups (e.g., no difference in entropy between SL and RL models; no difference in true positive rates between threshold and reward approaches; no difference in proportions).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in the metric being compared between the groups.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: A P value < 0.05 was regarded as statistically significant. The RL model significantly improved sensitivity for melanoma (P < 0.001) and basal cell carcinoma (P < 0.001) compared to supervised learning. The difference in entropy between SL and RL models was significant (P < 0.0001). There was no significant difference in true positive rates for melanomas between threshold and reward approaches (P = 0.11).

reinforcement_learning_model.entropy_reduction: Significant reduction in entropy compared to SL model (P < 0.0001, Wilcoxon test)

architecture_used.main_model: Deep Q-learning model (Reinforcement Learning) and Supervised Learning baseline (Convolutional Neural Network)
architecture_used.pretraining: The supervised learning model was fine-tuned from a previously described CNN for classification of the HAM10000 dataset (reference 14). The RL model's input features were derived from this supervised model.
architecture_used.input_size: One-dimensional state vector consisting of feature vectors and probabilities from the supervised model. For the patient-centered scenario, the input vector was normalized by dividing the lesion state vector position-wise by the average across all lesion vectors of the same patient.

architecture_details.rl_hidden_layers: A 256-unit fully connected layer with ReLU activation
architecture_details.rl_input_processing: Concatenation of the output of the 256-unit layer with the logits from the supervised model
architecture_details.rl_output_layer: Linear activation with units equal to the number of possible actions
architecture_details.rl_training_strategy: Exploration-exploitation strategy, replay buffer, and target Q-network with lower update rate

rl_hyperparameters.episode_length: Variable (250 to 12 for standard; varying based on number of lesions per patient for patient-centered)

classification_or_output_head.supervised_model: Convolutional Neural Network for classification of seven categories (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis/intraepidermal carcinoma, benign keratinocytic lesion, melanocytic nevus, dermatofibroma, vascular lesion)
classification_or_output_head.rl_model: Q-value prediction for actions (dismiss, monitor, treat locally, excise) based on predicted class probabilities and expert-defined rewards

preprocessing_techniques: Normalization of the input vector for the patient-centered scenario: the lesion state vector was divided position-wise by the average across all lesion vectors of the same patient to account for the context of multiple lesions.
Ordering of lesions according to malignancy probability inside the episode for the patient-centered model to improve performance.
conclusions: Incorporating human preferences via reinforcement learning (using nonuniform rewards and penalties based on expert-generated tables) improved AI-based decision support for skin cancer diagnosis compared to standard supervised learning.

The reinforcement learning model significantly increased sensitivity for melanoma from 61.4% to 79.5% (95% CI: 73.5–85.6%, P < 0.001) and for basal cell carcinoma from 79.4% to 87.1% (95% CI: 80.3–93.9%, P < 0.001) while maintaining high average accuracy (79.2%).
The reinforcement learning model reduced AI overconfidence by increasing the Shannon entropy of predictions, particularly for incorrect diagnoses, without negatively impacting average accuracy.

In a reader study with 89 dermatologists, AI support increased the rate of correct diagnoses by 12.0% (95% CI: 8.8–15.1%) and improved the rate of optimal management decisions from 57.4% to 65.3% (95% CI: 61.7–68.9%).

The reward-adjusted reinforcement learning model and a threshold-based model outperformed a naïve supervised learning approach in various clinical scenarios, including binary malignant vs. benign classification and patient-centered management of multiple lesions.
The study demonstrates the potential for incorporating human preferences into image-based diagnostic algorithms to balance the benefits and harms of diagnostic errors.
important_remarks: The reinforcement learning model was trained on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have class imbalance and potential selection bias, and was validated on the ISIC 2018 test set; external validation on diverse, real-world clinical populations beyond these specific benchmarks is limited.

The study relies on a reader study with 89 dermatologists to demonstrate clinical utility; results may be subject to observer bias or variability in expert judgment, and the generalizability to non-expert clinicians or different healthcare settings is not assessed.

The reward table used to train the RL model was generated by ten experts; the model's performance is thus contingent on the specific preferences and consensus of this expert panel, which may not reflect broader clinical guidelines or patient values.

The patient-centered scenario analysis used a private dataset from the Medical University of Vienna and a Vienna practice (n=7,375 lesions), which may not represent the global distribution of skin lesions or demographics, limiting external validity.

While the RL model improved sensitivity for melanoma and BCC, it did so by increasing prediction entropy (uncertainty); this trade-off between sensitivity and confidence requires careful interpretation in clinical deployment to avoid undermining clinician trust.

The study compares RL against a supervised learning baseline and threshold-based approaches but does not compare against state-of-the-art deep learning models trained with other advanced techniques (e.g., focal loss, advanced data augmentation) that might also address class imbalance.

The 'naive' and 'threshold' baselines in the patient-centered scenario were derived from expert-provided thresholds, which introduces variability and potential subjectivity in the comparison metrics for optimal management.
